steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The blank must join two independent clauses: ‘The sand at Balos Beach in Greece is a more unusual [shade], though’ and ‘deposits of crushed coral and other organic matter lend the sand a unique pink hue.’ The word ‘though’ functions as a postpositive adverb that ends the first clause; a semicolon must follow it to separate the two independent clauses.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'shade, though': A comma before the second independent clause creates a comma splice. ✗
  • Choice B — 'shade: though': A colon is used to introduce what follows, not to close a clause with ‘though.’ Incorrect here. ✗
  • Choice C — 'shade, though,': Treats ‘though’ as parenthetical by placing commas on both sides, which leaves the first independent clause without proper end punctuation. ✗
  • Choice D — 'shade, though;': The comma after ‘shade’ is part of the clause, and the semicolon after ‘though’ correctly separates the two independent clauses. ✓
That matches choice D — D is correct.
